title: Graph isomorphism in quasipolynomial time II
speaker: László Babai
abstract: The algorithm indicated in the title builds on Luks's classical framework and introduces new group theoretic and combinatorial tools. In the first talk we outline the algorithm and state the core group theoretic and algorithmic ingredients. Some of the technical details will be given in the second talk, with a focus on the core group theoretic routine ("Local Certificates") and the aggregation of the the local certificates. Time permitting, we also discuss one of the combinatorial partitioning algorithms ("Design lemma").

proof_stages.label: String-isomorphism formulation and group toolbox
proof_stages.summary: The graph-isomorphism task is reformulated as string isomorphism under an ambient permutation group, with isomorphism sets represented as cosets and standard polynomial-time permutation-group routines recalled as implicit infrastructure.

proof_stages.label: Luks divide-and-conquer by orbits and blocks
proof_stages.summary: The algorithm first exploits the ambient group's canonical orbit and imprimitivity-block structure to reduce the problem recursively, while controlling nonunique block-system choices by the n−1 bound on maximal systems and bounded individualization.

proof_stages.label: Quasipolynomial reduction blueprint
proof_stages.summary: The overall pipeline is previewed: local certificates on a hard set gamma are aggregated into a canonical logarithmic-arity relation, and then combinatorial reductions turn such structure into colorings, partitions, or Johnson-type structure at quasipolynomial cost.

proof_stages.label: Local test-set dichotomy and certificates
proof_stages.summary: For each logarithmic test set T in gamma, the algorithm asks whether the automorphism image is giant on T and produces either a global fullness certificate or a local non-fullness subgroup obstruction, with unaffected stabilizers explaining how local information yields global automorphisms.

proof_stages.label: Non-fullness aggregation to a canonical k-ary relation
proof_stages.summary: When non-fullness dominates, the local obstructions are compared over all ordered test tuples and across the two input strings to define a functorial k-ary relation on gamma with substantial symmetry defect.

proof_stages.label: Fullness aggregation via the generated automorphism group
proof_stages.summary: When fullness dominates, the fullness subgroups generate a canonical automorphism group F whose support, orbit partition, and transitivity level yield a good coloring or partition, or else reduce after few individualizations to a transitive non-giant action.

proof_stages.label: Invariant graph and downstream combinatorial closure
proof_stages.summary: The binary stage is reached either by the design/split-or-Johnson reductions or, in the fullness branch, by taking an orbital graph of a transitive but not doubly transitive action, giving the nontrivial invariant graph needed for the quasipolynomial recursion.

examples.name: Hexagon symmetries and two block systems
examples.note: The dihedral action on a hexagon, with the Star-of-David partition and opposite-pair partition, makes concrete that a transitive group can have several systems of imprimitivity.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites the hexagon symmetry group as having the Star of David and opposite-pair imprimitivity partitions, without analyzing or verifying them

The dihedral action on a regular hexagon is highly symmetric and is used to show that a transitive group need not have a unique imprimitivity system.
The regular hexagon’s full dihedral symmetry is highly symmetric and is used to exhibit two distinct imprimitivity systems, defeating uniqueness.

examples.name: Regular permutation representation and coset blocks
examples.note: The regular action of a group on itself illustrates how every subgroup gives a system of imprimitivity via its cosets, showing the possible abundance of block systems.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the regular permutation representation by right translations and applies the coset construction to get imprimitivity systems

passes.reason: the regular action has coset block systems from subgroups, exhibiting non-uniqueness of imprimitivity systems
the regular action is a sharply transitive systematic construction whose cosets illustrate the richness of block systems
The right-regular action is sharply transitive, and cosets of subgroups exhibit many imprimitivity systems rather than a unique one.
the regular action on a group is a uniform systematic construction whose cosets show that many block systems can occur
The regular action is sharply transitive and cosets of subgroups give many block systems, obstructing any unique block-system choice.
the regular action with subgroup coset blocks is used to illustrate many possible systems of imprimitivity rather than a unique one
The cosets in the right-regular action show nonunique block systems, and the action is the sharply transitive extremal representation of a group.
the regular action with coset blocks shows non-uniqueness of block systems and is a sharply transitive minimal transitive action
The regular action is sharply transitive, an extremal/minimal transitive action, and its coset block systems illustrate failure of uniqueness of imprimitivity systems.
The regular action of a group on itself is a homogeneous/sharply transitive construction whose subgroup cosets give many block systems, again showing non-uniqueness.
the right regular action is a sharply transitive, highly regular boundary case of transitive G-actions and its coset blocks show non-uniqueness of block systems
the regular action with coset block systems illustrates failure of uniqueness of imprimitivity systems, and regular actions are sharply transitive with minimal point stabilizers

examples.name: Elementary abelian group with equivalent cyclic subgroups
examples.note: The vector-space/Cayley-graph example shows that no single maximal imprimitivity system need be canonical, motivating the algorithmic move of individualizing one member of a canonical bounded set.
examples.engagement_reason: he invokes elementary abelian/vector-space groups only to state that their cyclic subgroups give equivalent maximal imprimitivity systems

passes.reason: an elementary abelian group has equivalent one-dimensional cyclic subgroups, obstructing a canonical choice of one block system

the elementary abelian vector-space case has all one-dimensional cyclic subgroups equivalent, obstructing a canonical choice of maximal imprimitivity system

examples.name: Individualizing one vertex versus a triple
examples.note: The speaker uses the cost n for choosing one vertex and n^3 for choosing a triple to illustrate how individualization produces multiplicative branching factors.
examples.engagement_reason: he computes the branching cost: individualizing one vertex costs n, while individualizing a triple costs n^3
